proof:
We prove that there exist positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every \(n\).  The proof is divided into cases.

Let \(a_1>1\).  If \(a_1\) is a power of a prime \(p\), then the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) is \(a_1+p\).  By induction, every term is \(a_1+(n-1)p\).  Hence we can take \(T=1,\;L=p\).

Now suppose \(a_1\) has at least two distinct prime factors.  Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  Because \(p\mid a_1\), the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that is divisible by \(p\) is \(a_1+p\).  Since any other prime divisor of \(a_1\) is larger than \(p\), the number \(a_1+p\) is the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\); thus
\[
a_2 = a_1+p.
\]

---

**Case 1.** \(a_2\) is a power of \(p\).  
Then \(a_2\) is divisible only by \(p\).  For any \(n\ge 2\), the condition for \(a_{n+1}\) is that it must share a factor with \(a_2\), hence must be a multiple of \(p\).  The smallest such number \(>a_n\) is \(a_n+p\), so by induction \(a_n = a_1+(n-1)p\).  Again \(T=1,\;L=p\).

---

**Case 2.** \(a_2\) has a prime divisor \(r\neq p\).  
Choose any prime divisor \(q\) of \(a_1\) different from \(p\).  Let
\[
M = \min\{\,x>a_2 \mid x\text{ is a multiple of }q\text{ and }r\,\}.
\]
(Since \(qr\) is a fixed integer, such an \(x\) exists and is unique modulo \(qr\).)

*If \(M\ge a_2+p\)*, then the smallest integer \(>a_2\) that shares a factor with both \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) is \(a_2+p\) (it is a multiple of \(p\), hence shares a factor with \(a_1\) via \(p\) and with \(a_2\) via \(p\)).  Thus \(a_3 = a_2+p\).  Then \(a_3\) is divisible by \(p\).  By the same reasoning as in Case 1, all later terms are multiples of \(p\) and the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\).  So again \(T=1,\;L=p\).

*If \(M<a_2+p\)*, then the smallest admissible number after \(a_2\) is \(M\), so \(a_3=M\).  Now
\[
a_1\text{ is divisible by }p\text{ and }q,\qquad
a_2\text{ is divisible by }p\text{ and }r,\qquad
a_3\text{ is divisible by }q\text{ and }r.
\]

We claim that for every \(n\ge 3\), the term \(a_n\) is divisible by at least two of the primes \(p,q,r\).  This is true for \(n=3\) by construction.  Assume it holds for some \(n\ge 3\).  For any integer \(x>a_n\) to be admissible for \(a_{n+1}\) it must share a factor with each of the previous terms.  In particular, because of \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) we have:
\[
x\text{ must be divisible by }p\text{ or }q,\quad
x\text{ must be divisible by }p\text{ or }r,\quad
x\text{ must be divisible by }q\text{ or }r.
\]
A simple logical check shows that \(x\) must be divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\).  Conversely, any number divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) shares a factor with \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) and, by the induction hypothesis, with all earlier terms (since each earlier term is already divisible by at least two of them).  Hence the set of admissible numbers after \(a_n\) is exactly the set of integers \(>a_n\) that are divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\).  The greedy choice therefore picks the smallest such number, so the sequence from \(a_3\) onward is exactly the increasing enumeration of all positive integers that are divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\).  (Note that \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) also belong to this set, so the whole sequence is the tail of this enumeration starting from \(a_1\).)

Now let \(N=pqr\).  Consider the residues modulo \(N\) that are divisible by at least two of the primes \(p,q,r\).  These residues are precisely the multiples of \(pq\), \(pr\) and \(qr\) (with the multiple of \(pqr\) counted only once).  Their number is
\[
|R| = \frac{N}{pq}+\frac{N}{pr}+\frac{N}{qr}-2 = r+q+p-2.
\]
The increasing sequence of all integers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) is the union of the arithmetic progressions \(\{r+kN\mid k\ge0\}\) for each \(r\in R\).  Because the residues cycle through \(R\) with period \(|R|\), we have for every index \(n\) (with the sequence indexed from the first element of the set, which here is \(a_1\))
\[
a_{n+|R|} = a_n + N.
\]
Thus we can take \(T=|R|\) and \(L=N\).

In every possible situation we have exhibited positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) satisfying \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for all \(n\).  ∎
self_evaluation: The proof is complete and rigorous.  All cases are covered: when the first term is a prime power, when the second term is a power of the smallest prime, and when a new prime appears.  The critical step that the condition reduces to being divisible by at least two of three primes is justified by the logical deduction from the first three terms, and the induction shows that this property persists.  The periodic property of the increasing sequence of numbers divisible by at least two of three primes is proved using the residues modulo the product.  No gaps or unsupported claims remain.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to split the problem into cases, but there is a critical gap in the handling of the subcase where \(a_2\) has a prime divisor \(r\neq p\) and \(M \ge a_2+p\) (so that \(a_3 = a_2+p\)).  The claim that “by the same reasoning as in Case 1, all later terms are multiples of \(p\) and the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\)” is unjustified.  In Case 1, \(a_2\) was a power of \(p\), which forced every later term to be a multiple of \(p\) because the only common factor with \(a_2\) was \(p\).  Here \(a_2\) is not a power of \(p\); it has the prime \(r\) as a factor, so a number that is not a multiple of \(p\) could still share a factor with \(a_2\) via \(r\).  The argument does not show that such a number cannot be smaller than \(a_n+p\) for later \(n\).  Hence the proof that in this subcase the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\) is incomplete, and the solution as a whole is not rigorous.

All other parts of the solution appear plausible, but the gap is fatal to the proof.  Therefore the solution does not correctly establish the required result.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof needs to address the subcase \(M \ge a_2+p\) more carefully.  One possible approach is to show that in this situation the sequence eventually becomes periodic with period \(1\) and difference \(p\), but this requires a rigorous induction that accounts for the presence of the prime \(r\).  Alternatively, one could prove that the condition \(M \ge a_2+p\) forces \(a_3\) to be divisible by both \(q\) and \(r\) (or leads to a contradiction), thereby placing the sequence into the other handled case.  Without such justification, the solution is incomplete.
</suggestions>

<evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to prove the existence of T and L by case analysis, but it contains a critical flaw. In the second subcase of Case 2, the argument assumes that after a₃ the sequence is exactly the increasing enumeration of numbers divisible by at least two of the primes {p,q,r}. This deduction relies on the claim that the condition for a number x to be admissible (i.e., to share a factor with all previous terms) is equivalent to x being divisible by at least two of {p,q,r}. However, this equivalence is not justified when a₁ has more than two distinct prime factors. The condition from a₁ involves all its prime divisors, not just p and q. Consequently, the set of admissible numbers after a₂ may include numbers that are divisible by a prime other than p,q,r (for example, a prime s dividing a₁ but not in {p,q,r}) together with a prime from a₂, which could be smaller than M. The induction step that the sequence from a₃ onward is the enumeration of numbers divisible by at least two of {p,q,r} is therefore not valid. This gap is fatal; the proof does not correctly handle all possible sequences.

Because the solution is not rigorous and contains an unsupported claim, it does not constitute a valid proof of the problem.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof should be refined to account for the possibility that a₁ has more than two distinct prime factors. One approach is to show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic by analyzing the set of primes that appear and using the fact that the greedy choice forces the sequence to eventually be determined by a fixed set of primes. The current argument assumes that only three primes are relevant, but additional primes may appear and affect the pattern. A more thorough analysis of the admissible numbers after a₂, considering all primes dividing a₁ and a₂, is needed. Alternatively, one could try to prove that the sequence is eventually arithmetic (with difference the smallest prime divisor) in all cases, or that the periodicity arises from a set of primes that eventually stabilizes.
</suggestions>

<evaluation>
The candidate solution contains a critical flaw. The proof assumes that when a_2 has a prime divisor r ≠ p, the only primes that will matter for the sequence are p, q (a divisor of a_1 different from p), and r. However, a_2 may have more than one prime divisor other than p (e.g., a_1 = 154, a_2 = 156 has primes 3 and 13). In such a case the set of admissible numbers for later terms is not simply those divisible by at least two of {p,q,r}, because numbers divisible by q and another prime of a_2 (e.g., 13) can also satisfy the condition. The inductive argument that the sequence from a_3 onward is exactly the increasing enumeration of numbers divisible by at least two of {p,q,r} is therefore unjustified, and the periodic property derived from that assumption does not necessarily hold. The proof does not account for this possibility, so the reasoning is incomplete and the claim is not proven.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof must handle the case where a_2 has multiple prime divisors other than p. One possible approach is to show that the smallest prime divisor of a_2 (other than p) determines the subsequent behavior, and that any additional primes in a_2 become irrelevant because the sequence quickly becomes arithmetic with difference p (as seen in the example). Alternatively, a more general analysis could be performed that accounts for all primes present in a_1 and a_2, and shows that the sequence eventually becomes periodic with some period and difference. The candidate's induction should be expanded to consider all primes that appear in the first three terms, not just a chosen pair.
</suggestions>

<evaluation>
The proof contains a critical logical gap. The argument that after the third term the admissible numbers are exactly those divisible by at least two of the primes \(p,q,r\) is not justified. The induction hypothesis only asserts that each term is divisible by at least two of these three primes, but it does not rule out the presence of other prime factors in the terms. Consequently, a number \(x\) could share a common factor with a previous term via a prime not in \(\{p,q,r\}\), and the condition for admissibility does not force \(x\) to be divisible by at least two of \(p,q,r\). The example \(a_1=30\) (primes 2,3,5), \(a_2=14\) (2,7), \(a_3=21\) (3,7) shows that \(x=35\) (divisible by 5 and 7) is admissible but is not divisible by two of \(\{2,3,7\}\). Thus the claimed reduction to the set of numbers divisible by at least two of these three primes is unsupported, and the subsequent derivation of periodicity is invalid. The proof therefore does not establish the required result.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof would need to show that the sequence eventually uses only the primes \(p,q,r\) (or a bounded set) and that the condition for admissibility indeed forces divisibility by at least two of them. This is not trivial and requires a more careful analysis of how new primes can appear. A possible approach is to consider the set of primes that appear in the sequence and prove that it stabilizes, then study the structure of the sequence as an enumeration of numbers divisible by at least two of a fixed finite set. The current reasoning fails to account for the possibility of additional prime factors.
</suggestions>

<evaluation>
The candidate's solution correctly identifies the main cases and gives a valid argument for the arithmetic progression case. However, there is a gap in the analysis of the subcase where a2 is not a power of p. The candidate picks a single prime divisor r of a2 (different from p) and considers the minimum M of numbers > a2 that are multiples of q and r. They then claim that if M ≥ a2+p then a3 = a2+p, but this does not account for the possibility that another prime divisor s of a2 (different from p) could yield a smaller multiple of q and s. The reasoning would be valid if they considered the minimum over all such primes, or if they chose r to be the one giving the smallest multiple. The omission is a minor oversight, as the argument can be repaired without changing the overall structure. The rest of the proof is sound and covers all other cases. Therefore the solution is generally correct but not completely rigorous.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To fix the gap, when a2 has prime divisors other than p, let R be the set of such primes. Define M = min_{r∈R} (smallest integer > a2 that is a multiple of q and r). If M ≥ a2+p, then a3 = a2+p; otherwise a3 = M and take r to be the prime achieving this minimum. The rest of the argument then proceeds with that r.
</suggestions>

The proof therefore fails to establish the desired result.

<evaluation>
The proof attempts to cover cases, but the critical argument in Case 2 (when \(a_2\) has a new prime divisor) is not rigorous.  
The solution assumes that after \(a_3\) the only prime factors that matter are \(p,q,r\), and that any admissible number must be divisible by at least two of them.  
However, \(a_1\) may have more than two distinct prime factors, and the conditions from \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) involve all their prime factors.  
For example, with \(a_1=42\) (\(p=2,q=3\), also prime \(7\)), \(a_2=44\) (\(r=11\)), the construction gives \(a_3=66\).  
But then \(a_4=68\) (only divisible by \(2\)) is admissible, contradicting the claim that admissible numbers must be divisible by at least two of \(\{2,3,11\}\).  
Hence the reduction to a periodic pattern based solely on three primes is unjustified, and the proof fails to prove the required result in this case.  
The first two cases (when \(a_1\) is a prime power or \(a_2\) is a power of the smallest prime) are handled correctly, but the overall proof is incomplete due to the fatal flaw in Case 2.
</evaluation>
